The most difficult CEO skill that you must master. Okay, so what I want you guys to understand is that by far the hardest skill that you must learn as a CEO, as a leader, is the skill of managing your own psychology. Okay, organizational design, communication, hiring, firing, promotions, they are nothing compared to keeping your mental space clean. Because usually the reason that you cannot or have trouble with any of the things I just listed is because your mentality is off. You're not keeping your mental space clean. Okay, and so I know a lot of you can probably relate to this statement, which is like, if things are actually going really well, we're growing, we're making more money, like there's tons of stuff going right, why do I feel like shit? Right? I hear a lot of people say this. Okay, you also are at a point where if you've started a business or you are in a leadership position, then you've accomplished a lot. You're aware and you're smart. Okay, and so you've gotten to a point where other people want to work for you, right? Therefore, you feel like you're doing a bad job does not feel usual to you. Right? Like you feeling like you don't know what you're doing and like you're failing all the time is not what you are used to feeling. Up until this point, you've been competent in everything you've done, right? You felt like you've always done a good job. Why is it that this feels so hard? Right? And so the first realization that you have to accept is that everybody learns how to be a CEO by being a CEO. Okay, so the only thing that prepares you for running a company is running a damn company. There is no training. There is no schooling for this. This is something that you learn on the fly. Okay, inevitably, this means that you will face a broad spectrum of problems that you do not know how to solve. Okay, and they will require a skill set that you don't have. And this is normal. And this is something that I feel like so many people are struggling with and so many people always struggle with, not just y'all gym owners, but everyone in business. It's like they can't accept the fact that it feels like this. You feel like you're doing a shit job most of the time. And that's what being a CEO is, and that's why most people don't do it. Right? And also, everybody on your team and around you will expect you to know and have these skills because you are the CEO. So you don't have the skills and everyone expects that you have the skills. Okay, it's terrible and it's a hilarious, ironic twist of fate. And going hand in hand, even if you grasp this concept, you also have to understand a second concept, which is that even if you know what you're doing, things will go wrong anyways. There are so many things that you don't have control over and there are so many things that can harm a business. There's existential threats. There's all sorts of stuff beyond your control. And so you have to accept the fact that even if you're a damn good CEO and you make best decisions, things are still going to go wrong. Okay? And why? Why is that the case? Why is it so hard? Why is it that things can still go wrong even if I'm doing my best? I'm doing a dang good job because you're in a multifaceted, human organization looking to compete and win in a competitive marketplace. That is not easy. That is hard. Okay? And so many times you feel like on most days, I would say five days of the week, four days of the week, you feel like your performance is probably two out of 10, right? And this is just due to the nature of who you are. You've been an achiever up until this point. And it's really hard to accept the fact that being a CEO and running a business, you're going to feel like you're a two out of 10 because you probably are two out of 10 a lot of days. But you have to accept it anyways because nobody's going to fire you. Okay? The ironic truth is that every single thing in your organization is your fault. There's not one freaking thing. And like if it comes down to the existential threats, it's like, well, you should have seen that coming because you should have done more research getting into this business, right? Everything's your fault. Okay? And so the rack that's shaking, the admin that isn't following up, your leads that cart came in fast enough, the Yelp reviews that went down, your coach that showed up late to the five AM. Literally, every problem is 100% your fault as the owner and as the CEO. Every hire and every decision that you ever made happened under your watch, which means it's your fault. Okay? So it's natural to feel like you're a two out of 10 and you're fucking up everything and it starts to wear on you, right? Like it's natural to feel that way, okay? And so here's where most CEOs go wrong and where most people steer wrong and why most people, you see a lot of people go into business and go back out and get a job. Okay? One, they take things too personally or two, they do not take things personally enough. Okay? Taking things too personally. It means you take every issue incredibly personally and seriously and then you personally attempt to fix it all. Okay? What does that result in? That result in you suppressing the team because you're like, I own everything, I make all the decisions, it's all my fucking fault. Sally, don't touch that, it's my fault, I even hired you and you didn't know this in the first place. Sally's like, what the frick, this is my job. Don't suppress your team because you take things too personally and because you're too emotionally invested. Okay? Or if you take things too personally, you end up feeling terrible on a daily basis and you can't even get out of bed. I can't tell you how many times I talk to entrepreneurs and business owners and CEOs who are like, I just don't want to get out of bed in the morning. It's because they take it so far this way, they're so emotionally attached, they're so hard on themselves that they can't even get out of bed. Okay? That's like the highest form of being a victim. You're just beating yourself up in your own head. That's not being a victor, that's being a victim of your own mind. Number two is you don't take things personally enough. Okay? What does that mean? In an attempt to mask the pain that what's going on in your business and what's going wrong, you take on an attitude of it's not so bad or it's not a big deal, like we're totally fine. It is what it is. I'm going to accept life is how life is. It's bullshit. Suddenly, no situation requires any urgency or attention. You're like, things will work out. Things always go the right way. No, you make them go the right way. There are certain things that require urgency and attention, especially in business, especially in 2019. And it also results in your team being extremely frustrated because you don't take situations to heart or you don't help support them to fix the problems. Right? And so your team feels like there's like say there's a terrible retention problem and you're like, yeah, you know what? I think you guys, we just got to do a better job and I think that 12% will go down. And your team's like, what the heck dude? Like, why aren't you utilizing resources? Why are you helping us? Like, do you not really feel like this is a big issue? Right? And your team starts to slowly lose buy-in because they don't feel like you're bought in. Right? So either you take things to personally or you don't think take things personally enough. Okay? So ideally, you want to be urgent but not psychotic. Okay? It's a really broad spectrum right there. And you also need to be able to aggressively and decisively without feeling emotionally guilty. Right? So you need to be able to make decisions aggressively, make decisions decisively and not feel guilty. And not feel guilty about them. Okay? Not feel guilty if you make the wrong decision. Not feel guilty if you mess up but just be like, all right, I messed up. What's the next decision I need to make? What's the decision I need to make to make this right? Okay? Because you're often going to find yourself making decisions because you made the wrong decision in the first place. And that's okay. Just stop being emotional about it. Right? And so if you can separate the importance and the urgency of these issues from how you feel, then you will avoid your team freaking out, feeling like crap, as well as you feeling like crap and freaking out. Okay? Oops. So if you can't accept and you can't deal with these facts, then you were in the wrong position. Being a CEO is hard. Okay? You are going to feel like you're messing up most of the time. And I'm telling you this because I see so many people who are like, I'm not doing something right. There's something wrong because I feel like crap. No, the thing that's wrong is your mindset. It is your mindset about it. It's the fact that you do not accept that this is the role you have chosen. Right? You got into it because of all the dreams they sold you. And the fact is being a CEO is hard. It doesn't feel like butterflies and rainbows every day. Right? It's like one of the hardest roles in the world. Okay? So feeling like a failure and coming close to losing it is normal in business and as a CEO. You're going to fail. You're going to feel like you're going to lose your business. You're going to feel like everyone's going to quit. You're going to feel like your culture has gone down. You're going to feel like you don't know how to get new clients. You're going to feel like the market changed too fast. You're going to feel like there's too much competition. This is normal. This is business. And this is the business that you signed up for. So I want you to know that that is normal and it's okay. And you just need to accept it. Right? So how do you master the skill of managing your state? How do you master this? How do you develop a better mindset around this? There are going to be fires burning all around you most of the time. And you have to be able to sit there and remain calm during that situation. Right? So everyone is different, but honestly, in my time overseeing this group and mentoring others, these techniques are honestly the most universally applicable. Okay. And they're very simple. It's just a matter of most people don't execute. Right? One, making friends and having friends. This sounds weird, right? But you're in a group of about 900 other people who can relate to you. Be a friend. You'll have a friend. Right? Friends, people with more friends have less stress. People who have other people to talk to these things about feel better. It's just the truth. Second, kind of relating to this, stay in your head and you're dead. But also, writing down these problems. Okay? Write down the problems that are in your head. Write down the things that you feel crappy about. Suddenly they become more manageable. Suddenly you can write down what you can do about these things that you feel so crappy about. Right? Third, focusing on the road, not the wall. Okay? What does that mean? It means there's a thousand things that can sink your ship, but you need to focus on where you're sailing. And you stop giving attention to everything that could go wrong and everything that can put a hole in your boat. So remember, bottom line, everyone wants to quit. Many will try to cope with drugs, alcohol, sex, Netflix, or some other self detrimental mechanism. Right? Okay? So a lot of people that you see, especially you hear these stories about like high level CEOs, high level business owners, and they went the wrong route. They ended up falling into drugs, alcohol, sex, whatever. They're all scandal. Okay? And then one day they quit and they claim that it was the stress of the job that drove them to the story themselves. So they had to quit. Like my job was so stressful. I had to drink every night to just deal with the pain. No. Like great CEOs face the pain. Great CEOs confront the hard stuff. Great CEOs deal with sleepless nights. Great CEOs don't quit. You quit because you let your mind become unmanageable. You did not quit because the job is unmanageable. And I just want to leave you with saved. Yeah.
